category_title: High-achieving students who dominate group interactions and exclude lower-performing peers
minimal_context: The student is reviewing a peer's contribution during a mixed-ability group work session in an introductory education course.
dialogue:
TUTOR: Please review the section your group member just completed for the project.

STUDENT: I always appreciate contribution of all members. However, I have to say I feel safer if the contribution comes from a good student. Whenever we receive products from a less able member, we need to spend time rechecking so it takes our time.

TUTOR: What are you going to do with their section?

STUDENT: I am turning to do my own work. I do not trust their contributions and I am being held back by slower teammates.

TUTOR: Have you tried talking to them when they express their ideas?

STUDENT: When they get stuck, I just 'jump into their mouth'. I go faster and I do not clearly explain information to the lower-achieving students.

options.A: Use the students' levels of engagement as a quick screening assessment to monitor participation during the mixed-ability group work sessions.
options.B: Closely observe the students' interests and current understandings each session, and then build group work activities for the next week based on those observations.
options.C: Transition the decision as to when, and for how long, to take a break from the tutor to the student during the group work sessions.
options.D: Teach interpersonal and small-group skills to promote cooperation; require students to help each other and facilitate each other's learning (e.g., listening, sharing ideas, respecting viewpoints, and resolving conflict).
